enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted streaming server that lets anyone publish live RTMP or SRT video to YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, Vimeo, and other platforms without recurring license fees.
enrichment.seo_title: Restreamer Review 2026: Free Open-Source Self-Hosted Streaming Server
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Restreamer by datarhei is a free, open-source, self-hosted live streaming server for RTMP and SRT publishing to YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, and Vimeo, with no license costs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Restreamer is a free and open-source, self-hosted live streaming server maintained by the datarhei project, which began around 2015 in Germany and is led by developers including Jan Stabenow and Sven Erbeck. Distributed with no ongoing license costs, its source is published on GitHub as datarhei/restreamer, and it is built to run on infrastructure the user fully controls rather than as a hosted SaaS product, which distinguishes it sharply from paid multistreaming platforms such as Restream.io. The software ships as a Docker or Podman container and runs on Linux, macOS, Windows, Raspberry Pi, and other single-board computers, with a Raspberry Pi 4 or newer recommended for reliable performance. Once deployed, Restreamer exposes a browser-based admin panel with a setup wizard that lets a user configure live inputs from OBS, IP cameras, USB devices, network sources, or a Raspberry Pi camera module, and then republish that stream via built-in RTMP and SRT servers to destinations like YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, and Vimeo, or to custom RTMP endpoints such as Wowza. It supports more than 50 video formats, GPU-accelerated encoding, HDMI output, free HTTPS certificates via Let's Encrypt, and built-in logging plus system and user monitoring, giving operators full data ownership since no video ever has to pass through a third-party cloud service. Because Restreamer is entirely self-hosted, there are no subscription tiers, seat limits, or feature paywalls of the kind found in commercial restreaming SaaS tools; the only costs are the hardware and bandwidth an operator supplies. This makes it popular with hobbyist streamers, churches, schools, community broadcasters, and privacy-conscious organizations that want to avoid recurring fees or vendor lock-in for a single always-on live feed or an appliance-style streaming box. The tradeoff is that Restreamer requires the user to manage their own server, networking, and uptime, and it lacks the built-in multistreaming-to-many-platforms-simultaneously dashboard, analytics, and customer support that paid SaaS competitors provide. Documentation and community support are available through the project's GitHub repository, Docker Hub image, and official docs site, and the project accepts community funding through Open Collective and Patreon. For anyone specifically searching for a no-cost, developer-friendly, open-source alternative to commercial restreaming services, Restreamer is one of the most established options, with an active codebase and a track record stretching back roughly a decade.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Restreamer is an open-source live streaming server developed by the datarhei project that packages FFmpeg-based ingest and republishing tools behind a simple web admin interface. It is designed to be self-hosted on a server, virtual machine, or single-board computer such as a Raspberry Pi, giving the operator complete control over their video data instead of routing it through a third-party cloud platform.

Because it runs under an open license with no recurring fees, Restreamer is often used as a free alternative to commercial restreaming SaaS products for operators who are comfortable managing their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Restreamer accepts input from OBS, IP cameras, USB and network video sources, and Raspberry Pi camera modules, then republishes the stream through built-in RTMP and SRT servers to platforms like YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, and Vimeo, or to any custom RTMP-compatible destination such as Wowza.

The software includes a setup wizard, a simplified admin panel, support for more than 50 video formats, GPU encoding, HDMI output, free Let's Encrypt HTTPS certificates, and basic logging and system monitoring, all packaged for deployment via Docker or Podman on Linux, macOS, Windows, or Raspberry Pi hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Restreamer is completely free for both private and commercial use, with no ongoing license costs, seat limits, or feature paywalls. Operators only pay for the hardware, hosting, and bandwidth needed to run the server themselves.

Because there is no vendor-hosted tier, total cost of ownership depends entirely on where and how the server is deployed, from a low-cost Raspberry Pi 4 at home to a cloud VM or on-premises server for organizations with heavier streaming needs.

features.title: Self-hosted architecture
features.description: Runs entirely on infrastructure the user controls, whether a Raspberry Pi, on-premises server, or cloud VM, so video never has to pass through a third-party service.

features.title: RTMP and SRT publishing
features.description: Built-in RTMP and SRT servers let operators receive a live feed from OBS or a camera and republish it to YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, Vimeo, or custom endpoints like Wowza.

features.title: Docker and Podman deployment
features.description: Ships as a container image that installs in minutes on Linux, macOS, Windows, Raspberry Pi, and other single-board computers.

features.title: Setup wizard and admin panel
features.description: A browser-based admin interface with a guided setup wizard makes configuring new live streams straightforward without command-line work.

features.title: Wide format and hardware support
features.description: Supports more than 50 video formats plus GPU encoding, HDMI output, and camera input from USB, network, virtual, and Raspberry Pi camera sources.

features.title: Free HTTPS via Let's Encrypt
features.description: Built-in support for free SSL certificates secures the admin panel and stream endpoints without extra configuration.

features.title: Logging and monitoring
features.description: Includes user and system monitoring plus logging so operators can track stream health and server performance.

features.title: No licensing costs
features.description: Distributed as open-source software with no subscription fees, watermarks, or usage caps for private or commercial use.

pros: Completely free for personal and commercial use with no recurring license fees
Full data ownership since streams run on infrastructure the operator controls
Lightweight enough to run on a Raspberry Pi for low-cost, always-on streaming appliances
Open-source codebase with an active GitHub community and Docker-based deployment
Supports multiple simultaneous input sources and more than 50 video formats
cons: Requires the user to manage their own server, networking, and uptime
No built-in customer support team; help comes from community docs and GitHub
Lacks the unified multi-destination dashboard and analytics of paid restreaming SaaS tools
Initial setup requires more technical comfort than a hosted, no-install product
Scaling to many simultaneous streams requires the operator to provision more hardware

faqs.question: Is Restreamer really free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Restreamer is open-source software with no ongoing license costs for private or commercial use; the only expenses are the hardware, hosting, and bandwidth an operator supplies.

faqs.question: Is Restreamer the same product as Restream.io?
faqs.answer: No. Restreamer (by the datarhei project) is a free, self-hosted, open-source streaming server, while Restream.io is a separate, unrelated paid SaaS multistreaming platform.

faqs.question: What hardware do I need to run Restreamer?
faqs.answer: Restreamer runs via Docker or Podman on Linux, macOS, Windows, and single-board computers; the project recommends a Raspberry Pi 4 or newer, though it also runs on earlier Raspberry Pi models.

faqs.question: Which platforms can I stream to with Restreamer?
faqs.answer: Restreamer can republish a live stream to YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, Vimeo, and other RTMP-compatible services such as Wowza.

faqs.question: Does Restreamer require an internet connection to set up?
faqs.answer: You need an internet connection to pull the Docker image and, typically, to reach streaming destinations, but the server itself can run on a local network or on-premises hardware.

faqs.question: Who maintains Restreamer?
faqs.answer: Restreamer is maintained by the datarhei open-source project, whose Restreamer and Core codebases are hosted publicly on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can I use Restreamer for commercial broadcasts?
faqs.answer: Yes, the license permits both private and commercial use at no cost.

use_cases.title: Hobbyist and community live streaming
use_cases.description: Individuals, churches, and community groups use Restreamer on low-cost hardware like a Raspberry Pi to run an always-on live feed to YouTube or Facebook without paying a monthly SaaS fee.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious self-hosted broadcasting
use_cases.description: Organizations that want full control of their video data deploy Restreamer on their own servers instead of routing streams through a third-party cloud platform.

use_cases.title: Low-cost streaming appliances
use_cases.description: Developers build dedicated streaming boxes or kiosks using Restreamer's Docker image on single-board computers for events, security cameras, or digital signage.

features: Self-hosted deployment via Docker or Podman
RTMP and SRT publishing to YouTube, Twitch, Facebook, Vimeo, and custom endpoints
No license fees for private or commercial use
Community support via GitHub and documentation

enrichment.short_description: Restaurant reservation and table-management platform owned by American Express, used by diners to book tables and by restaurants to manage reservations, waitlists, and guest data.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free for diners; subscription (ResyOS) for restaurants
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Resy Review 2026: Pricing, Features and How It Works for Restaurants
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Resy is American Express's restaurant reservation platform. See ResyOS pricing plans, features, and how it compares for diners and restaurant operators in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Resy is a restaurant reservation and table-management platform founded in 2014 in New York City by Ben Leventhal, Gary Vaynerchuk, and Michael Montero, with early backing from investors including Airbnb and Union Square Hospitality Group. American Express acquired Resy in 2019, and the platform has operated as part of Amex's dining and lifestyle business ever since, including through the widely used Amex Resy Credit built into certain American Express cards. On the consumer side, Resy is free to use: diners search, book, and manage restaurant reservations through the Resy website and mobile app, join waitlists, and receive notifications when hard-to-get tables open up. On the restaurant side, Resy operates as ResyOS, a paid subscription platform for table management, reservations, guest CRM, POS integrations, and analytics. Following American Express's 2024 acquisition of Tock for roughly 400 million dollars, Amex began folding Tock's restaurant base into Resy in 2026, expanding the combined network past 25,000 venues and adding features like event ticketing and prepayment tools that originated on Tock. As of 2026, ResyOS offers four subscription tiers for restaurants: Platform at 249 dollars per month (reservation and table management with POS integrations), Platform 360 at 399 dollars per month (adds advanced analytics and automated guest insights), and two Tock-powered tiers, Essential at 269 dollars per month and Premium at 399 dollars per month, which add event ticketing, prepayment handling, and, on Premium, POS integrations and premium support. All ResyOS tiers charge a flat monthly fee with no per-cover or per-reservation fee, which differentiates Resy's pricing model from competitors like OpenTable that can charge per-seated-diner fees. Common features across every tier include table and waitlist management, automated guest alerts, credit-card-backed reservation holds and cancellation fees, deposits, and chat or email support, while higher tiers add phone support, booking APIs, chargeback protection, and access to exclusive VIP booking groups. Resy is best known among consumers for its role in Amex's dining benefits program and for partnerships with high-demand restaurants in major U.S. cities, and among restaurant operators for its flat-fee pricing model as an alternative to commission-based reservation platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Resy is a restaurant reservation platform founded in 2014 in New York City and acquired by American Express in 2019. It serves two audiences: diners, who use the free Resy app and website to find and book tables, and restaurants, who subscribe to ResyOS for reservation, table, and guest-management tools.

In 2026, American Express has been integrating its Tock platform, acquired in 2024, into Resy, expanding the combined restaurant network past 25,000 venues and bringing event-ticketing and prepayment features that originated on Tock into the broader Resy product line.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: For diners, Resy offers reservation search and booking, waitlist notifications for hard-to-book restaurants, and integration with the Amex Resy Credit benefit available on certain American Express cards.

For restaurants, ResyOS provides table and floor management, automated guest alerts, credit-card-backed cancellation policies and deposits, POS integrations, guest CRM and analytics, and, on Tock-powered tiers, event ticketing and prepayment tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ResyOS charges restaurants a flat monthly subscription rather than a per-cover fee. As of 2026, plans include Platform at 249 dollars per month, Platform 360 at 399 dollars per month, Essential (Tock-powered) at 269 dollars per month with a 3 percent prepayment fee, and Premium (Tock-powered) at 399 dollars per month with a reduced 2 percent prepayment fee.
Resy remains free for consumers to search, book, and manage reservations through its app and website.

features.title: Free consumer booking
features.description: Diners can search, reserve, and manage restaurant tables at no cost through the Resy website and mobile app.

features.title: Flat-fee restaurant pricing
features.description: ResyOS charges restaurants a fixed monthly rate instead of a per-cover fee, unlike commission-based competitors.

features.title: Table and waitlist management
features.description: Restaurant staff manage floor plans, seating, and walk-in waitlists from a single dashboard.

features.title: Automated guest alerts
features.description: Automated alerts tell restaurants when a repeat or VIP guest books, and tell diners when a sold-out table opens up.

features.title: Deposits and cancellation protection
features.description: Credit-card-backed reservation holds, deposits, and cancellation fees help restaurants reduce no-shows.

features.title: POS and analytics integrations
features.description: Higher ResyOS tiers connect with point-of-sale systems and provide advanced guest and revenue analytics.

features.title: Tock-powered event ticketing
features.description: Following the Tock integration, Essential and Premium plans add ticketed prix-fixe events and prepayment collection.

features.title: American Express card benefits
features.description: Resy powers the Amex Resy Credit, a recurring dining statement credit available on select American Express cards.

pros: Flat monthly pricing for restaurants with no per-cover fees
Free for diners with a large network of well-known restaurants, especially in major U.S. cities
Backed by American Express, giving it strong brand recognition and card-linked benefits
Growing network following the 2026 integration of Tock's restaurant base
Automated no-show protection tools like deposits and card-backed holds
cons: Restaurant subscription fees start relatively high at 249 dollars per month regardless of covers seated
Network still skews toward restaurants in and around major cities
Amex Resy Credit eligibility rules for consumers have changed and require checking eligibility at time of booking
Ongoing Tock integration in 2026 has created some transitional complexity for restaurants and diners
Owned by American Express, which limits independence compared to standalone reservation platforms

faqs.question: Who owns Resy?
faqs.answer: American Express acquired Resy in 2019, and Resy has operated as part of Amex's dining and lifestyle business since then.

faqs.question: Is Resy free for diners?
faqs.answer: Yes, searching for and booking restaurant reservations through the Resy app and website is free for consumers.

faqs.question: How much does Resy cost restaurants?
faqs.answer: As of 2026, ResyOS plans for restaurants range from 249 to 399 dollars per month depending on the tier, with no per-cover fees.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Resy and Tock?
faqs.answer: Tock was a separate reservation platform that American Express acquired in 2024; Amex has been integrating Tock's restaurant network and features, like event ticketing, into Resy starting in 2026.

faqs.question: What is the Amex Resy Credit?
faqs.answer: It is a recurring statement credit offered on select American Express cards for dining at eligible Resy restaurants, subject to eligibility rules that can change.

faqs.question: When was Resy founded?
faqs.answer: Resy was founded in 2014 in New York City by Ben Leventhal, Gary Vaynerchuk, and Michael Montero.

faqs.question: Does Resy charge restaurants per reservation?
faqs.answer: No, ResyOS uses flat monthly subscription pricing rather than per-cover or per-reservation fees.

use_cases.title: Booking hard-to-get restaurant reservations
use_cases.description: Diners use Resy to search availability, join waitlists, and get notified when tables open at in-demand restaurants.

use_cases.title: Restaurant table and reservation management
use_cases.description: Restaurants subscribe to ResyOS to manage their floor plan, reservations, waitlists, and guest history from one system.

use_cases.title: Reducing no-shows with deposits
use_cases.description: Restaurants use Resy's card-backed holds, deposits, and cancellation fees to protect revenue from no-show diners.

enrichment.short_description: Low-code platform for building internal business applications quickly by connecting to databases and APIs with drag-and-drop components.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium / per-user subscription with custom Enterprise pricing
enrichment.seo_title: Retool Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Retool is a low-code platform for building internal tools fast. See 2026 pricing plans, key features, pros and cons, and how it compares for developer teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Retool is a low-code development platform for building internal business applications, founded in 2017 by CEO David Hsu and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Backed by investors including Sequoia Capital and Y Combinator, Retool has raised roughly 165 million dollars in funding and was valued at about 3.2 billion dollars as of recent reporting, with an estimated headcount that grew from around 300 to over 400 employees through 2026. The platform lets engineering teams build admin panels, dashboards, CRUD apps, and workflow tools by dragging and dropping pre-built UI components, such as tables, forms, buttons, and charts, and wiring them to data sources including PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, REST and GraphQL APIs, and dozens of other integrations, using a combination of visual editing and JavaScript or SQL for custom logic. Because internal tools are typically used by employees rather than customers, Retool is positioned as a way for software teams to skip building repetitive CRUD interfaces from scratch and instead assemble them in hours rather than weeks. Retool's 2026 pricing follows a per-builder, per-internal-user model across four tiers. The Free plan supports up to five builders and five end users at no cost, with a monthly workflow-run allowance and limited AI credits. The Team plan costs roughly 10 dollars per builder per month on annual billing (with a higher month-to-month rate), adds a staging environment and app release versioning, and increases workflow-run and AI-credit limits. The Business plan runs about 50 dollars per builder per month on annual billing (65 dollars month-to-month), adding audit logging, rich permission controls, embeddable portals, and unlimited environments. The Enterprise plan is custom-priced and adds SAML and OpenID Connect SSO, source control integration, custom branding, independent workspaces, and dedicated support; Retool has also offered enterprise customers signing by a 2026 deadline bonus AI credit allotments. Beyond its core builder product, Retool has expanded into Retool Workflows for backend automation, Retool Mobile for internal mobile apps, Retool Database for a built-in Postgres-compatible data store, and Retool AI features for embedding AI agents and app generation into internal tools. It is widely used by engineering and operations teams at startups and larger enterprises that need custom internal software, such as admin dashboards, support tools, and data-entry systems, without dedicating a full front-end team to build and maintain them from scratch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Retool is a low-code platform, founded in 2017 by David Hsu and headquartered in San Francisco, designed to help engineering teams quickly build internal tools such as admin panels, dashboards, and CRUD applications. Rather than building these interfaces from scratch, developers connect Retool to their existing databases and APIs and assemble the UI from pre-built components.

The company has raised roughly 165 million dollars from investors including Sequoia Capital and Y Combinator and was valued at around 3.2 billion dollars in recent funding rounds, with a headcount that has grown past 400 employees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Retool's core builder combines drag-and-drop UI components (tables, forms, charts, buttons) with a code layer supporting JavaScript, Python, and SQL for custom logic, and connects natively to databases like PostgreSQL and MySQL as well as REST and GraphQL APIs.

The platform has expanded beyond the original web app builder to include Retool Workflows for backend automation and scheduled jobs, Retool Mobile for internal mobile apps, Retool Database as a built-in data store, and AI features for embedding agents or generating app scaffolding.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Retool offers a Free plan for up to five builders and five end users, a Team plan at roughly 10 dollars per builder per month (annual billing), and a Business plan at roughly 50 dollars per builder per month (annual billing), each adding more workflow runs, AI credits, and administrative controls.

Enterprise pricing is custom and adds SSO, source control, custom branding, and dedicated support; internal end users beyond the included allotment are billed separately at a lower per-user rate on paid plans.

features.title: Drag-and-drop UI builder
features.description: Assemble internal app interfaces from pre-built components like tables, forms, charts, and buttons without writing front-end code from scratch.

features.title: Broad data source connectivity
features.description: Connects to PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, REST APIs, GraphQL, and dozens of other integrations out of the box.

features.title: Custom logic with code
features.description: Supports JavaScript, Python, and SQL directly inside the builder for custom transformations and business logic.

features.title: Retool Workflows
features.description: Automate backend processes and scheduled jobs visually, separate from the front-end app builder.

features.title: Retool Database
features.description: A built-in Postgres-compatible database for teams that need a data store without standing up separate infrastructure.

features.title: Retool Mobile
features.description: Extend internal tools to native mobile apps for field or on-the-go employees.

features.title: Enterprise access controls
features.description: Higher tiers add SSO, audit logging, granular permissions, and independent workspaces for larger organizations.

features.title: AI-assisted app building
features.description: Includes AI credits and features for generating app components and agents inside the builder.

pros: Dramatically speeds up building internal tools compared to custom front-end development
Wide range of native integrations with databases and APIs
Free tier makes it easy for small teams to start without upfront cost
Backed by strong funding and investor support, suggesting long-term product investment
Expanded product suite (Workflows, Database, Mobile, AI) covers more than just the original app builder
cons: Per-builder pricing can get expensive as engineering teams and internal-user counts grow
Enterprise features like SSO and audit logging require the custom-priced top tier
Requires some coding knowledge (JavaScript/SQL) for anything beyond basic layouts
As a hosted platform, teams take on dependency risk for business-critical internal tools
Business and Enterprise tier pricing requires talking to sales for exact costs

faqs.question: What is Retool used for?
faqs.answer: Retool is used to build internal business applications, such as admin panels, dashboards, and CRUD tools, by connecting to existing databases and APIs and assembling the interface from pre-built components.

faqs.question: Who founded Retool and when?
faqs.answer: Retool was founded in 2017 by David Hsu and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does Retool have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Retool's Free plan supports up to five builders and five end users at no cost, with limited workflow runs and AI credits.

faqs.question: How much does Retool cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start around 10 dollars per builder per month on the Team plan (annual billing), rising to roughly 50 dollars per builder per month on the Business plan, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: What data sources can Retool connect to?
faqs.answer: Retool connects to databases like PostgreSQL, MySQL, and MongoDB, as well as REST and GraphQL APIs and dozens of other native integrations.

faqs.question: Does Retool support mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, Retool Mobile lets teams build and deploy internal tools as native mobile apps.

faqs.question: Is Retool only for developers?
faqs.answer: Retool is primarily designed for developers and technical builders, since custom logic uses JavaScript, Python, or SQL, though non-technical staff can be added as end users of the finished apps.

use_cases.title: Internal admin panels and dashboards
use_cases.description: Engineering teams build admin tools to manage customers, orders, or content without dedicating front-end resources to custom builds.

use_cases.title: Operations and support tooling
use_cases.description: Operations and customer support teams get purpose-built internal apps for tasks like refund processing or account lookups, connected directly to production databases and APIs.

use_cases.title: Workflow automation
use_cases.description: Teams use Retool Workflows to automate backend processes, such as data syncing or scheduled reporting, alongside their internal apps.

features: Up to 5 builders and 5 end users

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, decentralized platform for encrypted chat, file sharing, forums, and voice/video calls built directly between trusted friend-to-friend nodes.
enrichment.seo_title: RetroShare Review 2026: Free Decentralized Encrypted Communication Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: RetroShare is a free, open-source, decentralized platform for encrypted chat, file sharing, forums, and calls between trusted friend-to-friend nodes. See features and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RetroShare is a free, open-source, decentralized communication platform that was founded in 2006 by a developer known by the handle drbob to enable secure, private communication and file sharing directly between trusted friends, without relying on centralized servers. Over time additional contributors joined the project, including a developer known as Crypton who built the Qt-based graphical interface still used today, and the software has continued under active open-source development for roughly two decades. RetroShare works by establishing encrypted connections, secured with OpenPGP and OpenSSL, directly between a user and people they explicitly add as friends, forming a distributed friend-to-friend network rather than a client-server system or a fully open peer-to-peer swarm like BitTorrent. On top of that encrypted network, RetroShare layers several distributed services: private and group chat, including distant chat that can reach friends-of-friends through anonymous tunnels; forums and boards that sync automatically between friends and can be read and written offline, making them resistant to censorship since there is no central server to shut down; a mail-like private messaging system that stores messages on friends' nodes so they can be delivered even while a recipient is offline; file sharing that uses swarming techniques similar to BitTorrent to speed up transfers of large files; and, through an optional VoIP plugin, encrypted voice and video calls between connected peers. Because only direct friends can see what a user shares or downloads, and traffic to more distant network members is anonymized through tunneling, RetroShare is positioned as a privacy- and censorship-resistant alternative to mainstream chat and file-sharing platforms. It is entirely free and open source, with no subscription fees, no advertising, and no formal terms of service, and it is available on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android. As a community-maintained project rather than a commercial company, RetroShare has no headquarters, employee count, or revenue model in the conventional sense; its ongoing development instead relies on volunteer contributors coordinating through GitHub and community documentation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RetroShare is an open-source, decentralized communication platform founded in 2006 by a developer known as drbob, designed to let people communicate and share files securely and privately with friends they explicitly trust, without routing traffic through centralized servers.
The project is entirely community-maintained and free, distributed for Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android, with development coordinated publicly on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RetroShare combines encrypted chat (including distant chat to friends-of-friends), decentralized forums and boards that sync offline, a store-and-forward private messaging system, BitTorrent-style swarming file sharing for large files, and an optional VoIP plugin for encrypted voice and video calls.
All connections are secured using OpenPGP and OpenSSL, and traffic beyond a user's direct friends is routed through anonymous tunnels, so only direct friends can see what a user shares or downloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RetroShare is completely free and open source, with no subscription fees, in-app purchases, advertising, or hidden costs of any kind.

Because it is a volunteer-maintained project rather than a commercial product, there are no paid tiers, enterprise plans, or support contracts; users rely on community documentation and forums for help.

features.title: Friend-to-friend encrypted network
features.description: Builds direct encrypted connections only with explicitly trusted friends, secured with OpenPGP and OpenSSL.

features.title: Decentralized forums and boards
features.description: Censorship-resistant forums sync automatically between friends and can be read and written offline.

features.title: Distant chat via anonymous tunnels
features.description: Lets users securely message friends-of-friends through the network using anonymous routing.

features.title: Store-and-forward messaging
features.description: Private messages are stored on friends' nodes so they can still be delivered when a recipient comes back online.

features.title: Swarming file sharing
features.description: Uses BitTorrent-like swarming to speed up transfers of large files between friends or the wider network.

features.title: Optional VoIP plugin
features.description: Adds encrypted voice and video calling between connected peers.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Available on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android.

features.title: No central server
features.description: Fully decentralized architecture means there is no company-run server that can be shut down or subpoenaed for user data.

pros: Completely free with no subscription or hidden costs
Strong privacy model based on friend-to-friend encryption rather than centralized servers
Censorship-resistant forums and messaging with offline sync
Open-source codebase that anyone can audit
Supports large file sharing through swarming technology
cons: Requires manually adding and verifying friends, which limits network reach compared to open platforms
No official company support; help comes only from community documentation and forums
Interface and setup are more technical than mainstream chat apps
Network effects are weaker since you can only reach people through friend connections
Development pace and updates depend entirely on volunteer contributors

faqs.question: Is RetroShare really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, RetroShare is fully free and open source with no subscription fees, ads, or hidden costs.

faqs.question: How is RetroShare different from Tor or VPNs?
faqs.answer: RetroShare builds encrypted connections directly between trusted friends rather than anonymizing traffic through a shared relay network, forming a friend-to-friend rather than fully anonymous network.

faqs.question: Who created RetroShare?
faqs.answer: RetroShare was founded in 2006 by a developer known by the handle drbob, with later contributions from other developers including one known as Crypton, who built the Qt graphical interface.

faqs.question: What platforms does RetroShare support?
faqs.answer: RetroShare is available on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android.

faqs.question: Can I share files with people I'm not directly connected to?
faqs.answer: Yes, RetroShare uses anonymous tunnels so users can reach friends-of-friends for chat and content, while still protecting the identity of who is sharing what.

faqs.question: Does RetroShare have a company behind it?
faqs.answer: No, RetroShare is a volunteer-maintained open-source project with no corporate entity, headquarters, or formal support contracts.

faqs.question: Is RetroShare good for large file transfers?
faqs.answer: Yes, it uses swarming techniques similar to BitTorrent, which is well suited to sharing large files efficiently among friends.

use_cases.title: Private encrypted group communication
use_cases.description: Small trusted groups use RetroShare for encrypted chat, calls, and file sharing without relying on a company-run server.

use_cases.title: Censorship-resistant forums

enrichment.short_description: AI-powered eDiscovery, document review, legal hold, and investigations platform used by corporate legal teams, law firms, and government agencies.
enrichment.seo_title: Reveal (RevealData) Review 2026: AI eDiscovery Platform Features and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Reveal (RevealData) is an AI-powered eDiscovery and document review platform for legal teams. See its history, Brainspace merger, key features, and pricing model.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Reveal, operating as RevealData, is an AI-powered eDiscovery, document review, legal hold, and investigations platform headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, with roots dating back to 2009 under founder and CEO Wendell Jisa. The company's current form took shape in January 2021, when Reveal merged with Brainspace, an artificial intelligence and analytics engine used across eDiscovery, cybersecurity, and fraud investigation workflows; the combined company was backed by more than 200 million dollars in investment from K1 Investment Management. Reveal had previously grown through acquisitions of Mindseye in 2019 and NexLP in 2020, folding additional AI, analytics, and workflow capabilities into its platform ahead of the Brainspace merger. Today Reveal serves corporate legal departments, law firms, legal service providers, government agencies, and educational institutions, reporting more than 4,000 customers across over 50 countries, with a workforce that industry data places at roughly 500 employees and estimated annual revenue in the hundreds of millions of dollars. The Reveal platform covers the full eDiscovery Reference Model, from identifying and preserving data, through collection, processing, review, and analysis, to production and courtroom or hearing presentation. Its most prominent recent capability is a generative AI layer called aji, paired with a natural-language query tool called ASK that lets legal teams interrogate large document sets directly in plain English, and a structured AI-powered document review workflow that applies predictive coding, concept clustering, and relevance scoring at scale. Reveal also maintains a library of more than 25 pre-built AI models tuned for specific legal scenarios, such as antitrust investigations, personally identifiable information detection, and HR matters, which legal teams can use out of the box or customize for their own case data. Because Reveal sells to enterprise legal and corporate customers with highly variable data volumes and case complexity, it does not publish self-serve pricing; prospective customers get quotes tailored to data volume, user count, and specific modules needed, typical of enterprise legal technology and eDiscovery software. It is important not to confuse Reveal/RevealData, the legal AI and eDiscovery company, with reveal.js, an unrelated open-source HTML presentation framework that happens to share a similar name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Reveal, marketed under the RevealData brand, is an AI-powered eDiscovery and legal investigations platform headquartered in Chicago, Illinois. The company traces back to 2009 under founder and CEO Wendell Jisa, and took its current shape in January 2021 when Reveal merged with the AI analytics company Brainspace, backed by more than 200 million dollars from K1 Investment Management.

Reveal serves corporate legal departments, law firms, legal service providers, and government agencies, reporting over 4,000 customers across more than 50 countries and a workforce of roughly 500 employees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Reveal covers the entire eDiscovery Reference Model, from legal hold and data preservation through collection, processing, review, analysis, and production, and includes tools for presenting evidence at hearings and trials.

Its generative AI layer, aji, works alongside a natural-language query tool called ASK and a structured AI-powered document review workflow using predictive coding and concept clustering, plus a library of more than 25 pre-built AI models for common legal scenarios like antitrust investigations and PII detection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Reveal does not publish self-serve pricing; as an enterprise eDiscovery platform, costs are quoted based on data volume, number of users, and the specific modules a legal team or law firm needs.
Prospective customers work directly with Reveal's sales team to scope pricing for a given matter, case, or ongoing subscription relationship.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source HTML presentation framework that lets anyone build interactive, code-based slide decks using web technologies.
enrichment.seo_title: reveal.js Review 2026: Free Open-Source HTML Presentation Framework
enrichment.seo_meta_description: reveal.js is a free, MIT-licensed, open-source HTML presentation framework by Hakim El Hattab. See features, plugins, and how it compares to slide software.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: reveal.js is a free, open-source HTML presentation framework created by Swedish front-end developer and interface designer Hakim El Hattab, who first released it in 2011 and continues to maintain it today. Distributed under the MIT license through its GitHub repository (hakimel/reveal.js) and npm package, reveal.js lets anyone with a web browser build fully-featured, interactive slide presentations using standard web technologies rather than proprietary slide software. Because a reveal.js deck is just an HTML page, presenters can do anything on a slide that they could do on any web page, including styling with custom CSS, embedding external pages via iframes, pulling in live data, or adding custom behavior through reveal.js's JavaScript API. Core features include nested (vertical and horizontal) slides, Markdown support for writing slide content in plain text, an Auto-Animate feature that smoothly transitions matching elements between slides, PDF export for offline sharing or printing, speaker notes with a dedicated presenter view that includes a timer and a preview of the upcoming slide, LaTeX typesetting for mathematical content, syntax-highlighted code blocks, and an extensive plugin and API ecosystem for customization. Presentations built with reveal.js are responsive by default, scaling to fit different screen sizes and devices, and support custom themes, transition styles, parallax backgrounds, and configurable keyboard bindings. Because it requires no installation for viewers, a reveal.js presentation can be shared as a static HTML page or bundled into a build pipeline with tools like Webpack or Vite for larger projects. The npm package has accumulated tens of thousands of GitHub stars, reflecting its status as one of the most widely used open-source tools among developers, technical conference speakers, and educators who prefer building slides in code rather than in dedicated slide-design software. It is completely free for any use, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or company behind it, distinguishing it entirely from RevealData/Reveal, an unrelated commercial legal eDiscovery platform that happens to share a similar name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: reveal.js is an open-source HTML presentation framework created by developer Hakim El Hattab, who released the first version in 2011 and still actively maintains the project. It is distributed under the MIT license and lets anyone build interactive, code-based slide presentations that run in any web browser.

Because a reveal.js presentation is fundamentally an HTML page, presenters have full access to web technologies like CSS, JavaScript, and iframes to customize and extend their slides in ways not possible in traditional slide software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: reveal.js supports nested vertical and horizontal slides, Markdown-based slide authoring, an Auto-Animate feature for smooth element transitions, PDF export, LaTeX typesetting, and syntax-highlighted code blocks.

It also includes a dedicated speaker view with a timer and next-slide preview, custom themes and transitions, parallax backgrounds, configurable keyboard bindings, and an extensive JavaScript API and plugin system for further customization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: reveal.js is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees of any kind.

Because it is distributed as a code library rather than a hosted product, the only potential costs come from optional hosting for published presentations, which developers can handle through any static web host of their choice.

features.title: Extensive JavaScript API
features.description: A rich API and plugin ecosystem let developers customize behavior, add interactivity, or embed live content.

features.title: Responsive, web-native slides
features.description: Presentations scale to fit any screen and can include anything a normal web page can, including embedded iframes and custom CSS.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing costs
Highly customizable since presentations are built from standard HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
Popular among developers and technical speakers for code-based, version-controllable slide decks
Strong built-in support for code syntax highlighting and LaTeX, ideal for technical content
Large, long-running open-source community with tens of thousands of GitHub stars
cons: Requires comfort with HTML, CSS, and optionally JavaScript, unlike drag-and-drop slide tools
No built-in cloud collaboration features found in commercial presentation software
Design polish depends on the author's own CSS and theme choices rather than a built-in design assistant
No official commercial support; help comes from community docs and the open-source repository
Steeper learning curve for non-technical presenters compared to tools like PowerPoint or Google Slides

faqs.question: Is reveal.js free?
faqs.answer: Yes, reveal.js is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: Who created reveal.js?
faqs.answer: reveal.js was created by developer Hakim El Hattab, who first released it in 2011 and continues to maintain it.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to use reveal.js?
faqs.answer: Basic slide decks can be written in Markdown with minimal coding, but customizing behavior or advanced layouts benefits from knowledge of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

faqs.question: Is reveal.js the same as Reveal/RevealData?
faqs.answer: No. reveal.js is an unrelated, unaffiliated open-source HTML presentation framework; Reveal/RevealData is a separate commercial legal eDiscovery and AI document review company.

faqs.answer: Yes, reveal.js includes built-in PDF export for offline sharing or printing.

faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a dedicated speaker view with notes, a timer, and a preview of the upcoming slide.

faqs.question: Where is reveal.js hosted?
faqs.answer: The reveal.js source code is hosted on GitHub under hakimel/reveal.js and distributed via npm.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based iPad point-of-sale platform for restaurants and retail, offering POS, inventory, employee management, and multi-location reporting.

enrichment.headquarters: Atlanta, Georgia, United States (originally founded in San Francisco, California)
enrichment.seo_title: Revel Systems Review 2026: iPad POS Pricing, Features and Shift4 Acquisition
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Revel Systems is a cloud-based iPad POS platform for restaurants and retail, now owned by Shift4. See 2026 pricing, features, contract terms, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Revel Systems is a cloud-based, iPad-native point-of-sale platform for restaurants and retail businesses, founded in 2010 in San Francisco by Lisa Falzone and Chris Ciabarra as one of the earliest companies to build a POS system specifically around Apple's iPad hardware. Over the following years the company relocated its headquarters to Atlanta, Georgia, while maintaining additional offices including one in San Francisco, and grew into a multi-location restaurant and retail management platform used across thousands of merchant locations. In June 2024, payments company Shift4 acquired Revel Systems for approximately 245 million dollars, folding Revel into Shift4's broader payments and commerce technology portfolio; that acquisition has introduced some uncertainty in the market about Revel's long-term roadmap as a standalone product brand. Revel's core platform combines point-of-sale software with inventory management, employee scheduling and management, customer relationship management, and reporting and analytics, marketed toward businesses that need strong multi-location management and reliable offline operation when internet connectivity drops. Pricing starts at approximately 99 dollars per month per terminal on annual billing, with a minimum of two terminals typically required and a standard three-year contract commitment that includes aggressive early-termination fees, a structure that differs from many newer, more flexible POS competitors that offer month-to-month plans. Beyond the base software subscription, Revel charges additional fees for features such as API access, extra kitchen display system (KDS) screens, advanced inventory counting tools, and enterprise-level multi-location reporting, so the effective monthly cost for a full-featured deployment is often higher than the advertised starting price. Revel is generally positioned toward mid-size and larger restaurant and retail operators who need robust multi-location and offline capabilities rather than very small single-location businesses, given its contract length and add-on fee structure. As with any recently-acquired platform, prospective customers evaluating Revel in 2026 should confirm current contract terms, pricing, and product roadmap directly with Revel or Shift4, since acquisition integrations can shift pricing structures, supported features, and long-term support commitments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Revel Systems is a cloud-based, iPad-native point-of-sale platform founded in 2010 in San Francisco by Lisa Falzone and Chris Ciabarra, among the first companies to build a POS system specifically for Apple's iPad. The company's headquarters later moved to Atlanta, Georgia, while it maintained a presence in San Francisco as it grew into a multi-location restaurant and retail platform.
In June 2024, payments company Shift4 acquired Revel Systems for approximately 245 million dollars, bringing Revel's POS technology into Shift4's broader payments portfolio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Revel's platform combines point-of-sale software with inventory management, employee scheduling, customer relationship management, and multi-location reporting and analytics, built for both restaurant and retail environments.

It is known for strong offline capabilities, letting locations keep processing sales even when internet connectivity drops, along with support for kitchen display systems and API access for custom integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Revel Systems pricing starts at approximately 99 dollars per month per terminal on annual billing, typically requiring a minimum of two terminals and a three-year contract with early-termination fees.

Additional features, such as API access, extra kitchen display screens, advanced inventory counting, and enterprise-level multi-location reporting, are billed as add-ons on top of the base subscription.

cons: Requires a three-year contract with aggressive early-termination fees
Minimum two-terminal requirement raises the effective entry cost
Many features, like API access and extra KDS screens, cost extra on top of the base subscription
Product roadmap uncertainty following the 2024 Shift4 acquisition

faqs.question: When was Revel Systems founded?
faqs.answer: Revel Systems was founded in 2010 in San Francisco by Lisa Falzone and Chris Ciabarra.

faqs.answer: Pricing starts at roughly 99 dollars per month per terminal on annual billing, typically requiring a minimum of two terminals and a three-year contract.

faqs.answer: Yes, Revel is known for its offline mode, which lets locations keep processing sales when internet connectivity drops.

faqs.answer: Yes, features like API access, additional kitchen display screens, and enterprise-level reporting are typically billed as add-ons.

enrichment.short_description: Backend-as-a-service platform that unifies in-app purchase and subscription management across iOS, Android, and the web for mobile app developers.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium / revenue-share (percentage of tracked revenue above a free threshold), with custom Enterprise pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Free up to $2,500 in monthly tracked revenue, then 1% above that threshold
enrichment.seo_title: RevenueCat Review 2026: Pricing, Features and How It Works
enrichment.seo_meta_description: RevenueCat is a backend platform for managing in-app purchases and subscriptions across iOS, Android, and the web. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RevenueCat is a backend-as-a-service platform for managing in-app purchases and subscriptions, founded in 2017 by Jacob Eiting and Miguel Carranza and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Backed by Y Combinator and reporting a headcount in the range of roughly 150 to 170 employees, RevenueCat has grown to power subscription infrastructure for more than 30,000 apps. The platform's core purpose is to abstract away the complexity of Apple's StoreKit and Google Play Billing, giving mobile developers a single, unified API and dashboard to manage purchases, entitlements, and subscriber data across iOS, Android, and the web, instead of building and maintaining separate billing integrations for each platform. Beyond the core subscription backend, RevenueCat's product suite includes open-source SDKs for all major platforms, a comprehensive REST API, pre-built integrations with mobile measurement partners (MMPs) for real-time purchase attribution, A/B testing with remote configuration, a native paywall editor with templates for building and iterating on paywalls without app-store resubmission, audience segmentation and targeting, a dashboard covering more than 40 monetization metrics, automated subscription support tooling, cross-platform customer insights, promotional subscription management, and event-based lifecycle campaign triggers. RevenueCat's 2026 pricing follows a straightforward revenue-share model rather than traditional seat licenses: the Pro plan is free for apps generating up to 2,500 dollars in monthly tracked revenue (MTR), after which RevenueCat charges 1 percent of tracked revenue above that threshold, with no per-transaction fees, seat licenses, or enterprise minimums built into the base structure. RevenueCat also offers an alternative Growth Tools pricing path for teams that want to use their own backend infrastructure and only pay a 1 percent MTR fee on conversions driven through specific tools like the paywall editor or web-to-app funnels. For companies with high transaction volume or specialized requirements, RevenueCat offers a custom-priced Enterprise plan that adds volume discounts, flexible tracking configurations, dedicated support, and custom SLAs. This pricing model, which scales with an app's actual revenue rather than charging flat fees regardless of usage, has made RevenueCat particularly popular among indie developers and early-stage mobile startups, while its analytics, experimentation, and paywall tools have also attracted larger app publishers looking to consolidate subscription infrastructure and monetization experimentation into a single platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RevenueCat is a backend-as-a-service platform for in-app purchases and subscriptions, founded in 2017 by Jacob Eiting and Miguel Carranza and headquartered in San Francisco, California. It gives mobile developers a unified API for managing purchases and entitlements across iOS, Android, and the web instead of building separate integrations for Apple's StoreKit and Google Play Billing.
Backed by Y Combinator, RevenueCat has grown to power subscription infrastructure for more than 30,000 apps, with a team of roughly 150 to 170 employees as of recent reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RevenueCat's core product includes open-source SDKs, a unified subscription backend, a REST API, and pre-built MMP integrations for real-time purchase attribution across platforms.

Additional tools include A/B testing with remote configuration, a native paywall editor with templates, audience segmentation, a dashboard with more than 40 monetization metrics, and event-based lifecycle campaign triggers for retention and win-back flows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RevenueCat's Pro plan is free for apps with up to 2,500 dollars in monthly tracked revenue, then charges 1 percent of tracked revenue above that threshold, with no seat licenses or per-transaction fees.

An alternative Growth Tools pricing path lets teams use their own backend and pay 1 percent of MTR only on conversions driven by specific tools like the paywall editor, while a custom-priced Enterprise plan adds volume discounts, dedicated support, and custom SLAs for high-volume apps.

Saves significant engineering time by abstracting Apple and Google billing complexity

Open-source SDKs and a widely adopted platform, used by more than 30,000 apps
cons: 1 percent revenue-share fee on high-revenue apps can become a meaningful cost at scale
Enterprise pricing requires a custom sales conversation for high-volume or specialized needs
Adds a third-party dependency for subscription infrastructure that is critical to app revenue
Growth Tools pricing path requires managing your own backend infrastructure
Primarily focused on mobile subscription apps, less relevant for non-subscription business models

faqs.question: What does RevenueCat do?
faqs.answer: RevenueCat is a backend-as-a-service platform that unifies in-app purchase and subscription management across iOS, Android, and the web, abstracting away Apple StoreKit and Google Play Billing complexity.

faqs.question: How much does RevenueCat cost?
faqs.answer: RevenueCat's Pro plan is free for up to 2,500 dollars in monthly tracked revenue, then charges 1 percent of tracked revenue above that threshold, with custom Enterprise pricing for larger apps.

faqs.question: Who founded RevenueCat?
faqs.answer: RevenueCat was founded in 2017 by Jacob Eiting and Miguel Carranza and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does RevenueCat charge per transaction?
faqs.answer: No, RevenueCat's core pricing is based on a percentage of monthly tracked revenue rather than per-transaction fees or seat licenses.

faqs.question: How many apps use RevenueCat?
faqs.answer: RevenueCat reports powering subscription infrastructure for more than 30,000 apps.

faqs.question: What is RevenueCat's paywall editor?
faqs.answer: It is a native tool for building and updating in-app paywalls with templates and remote configuration, without needing an app-store resubmission for changes.
